title: Start Here
summary: This wiki is a growing knowledge base on frontier AI, agentic software development, policy framing, and the emerging market structure around technical-work agents. It is designed for fast navigation through linked pages
text:
# Start Here

## What this wiki is
This wiki is a growing knowledge base on frontier AI, agentic software development, policy framing, and the emerging market structure around technical-work agents. It is designed for fast navigation through linked pages rather than long chronological notes.

## Best way to read it
- Open the folder in Obsidian at /home/gazaz/wiki.
- Start with index.md for the catalog.
- Use backlinks and the graph view to follow themes.
- Use topic-map when you want a thematic view rather than an alphabetical one.
- Check log.md when you want the newest additions.

## Recommended reading paths

### If you care about agentic coding platforms
1. codex-vs-claude-code-vs-hermes-agent-architecture-trust-moats
2. codex-vs-claude-code-and-open-agent-platforms
3. codex
4. claude-code
5. hermes-agent

### If you care about OpenAI and the Codex thesis
1. openai-codex-agentic-ai-future-2026-04-19
2. codex
3. agentic-software-development
4. superintelligence-governance

### If you care about AI policy framing
1. anthropic-google-openai-us-government-ai-policy-framings
2. openai
3. anthropic
4. industrial-policy-for-the-intelligence-age

## Current major themes
- Agentic coding: agentic-software-development, codex, claude-code, hermes-agent, openclaw
- Policy and governance: openai, anthropic, superintelligence-governance, industrial-policy-for-the-intelligence-age
- Comparative synthesis: codex-vs-claude-code-and-open-agent-platforms, codex-vs-claude-code-vs-hermes-agent-architecture-trust-moats, anthropic-google-openai-us-government-ai-policy-framings

## Navigation notes
- Entity pages explain who/what something is.
- Concept pages explain ideas and frameworks.
- Comparison pages are often the fastest way to understand strategic differences.
- Query pages capture substantial one-off research answers worth preserving.

title: Anthropic
summary: Anthropic is a frontier AI lab/company that, in this source, frames AI policy primarily through economic uncertainty, labor-market transition, and scenario planning rather than through a single fixed industrial-policy bl
text:
# Anthropic

## Summary
Anthropic is a frontier AI lab/company that, in this source, frames AI policy primarily through economic uncertainty, labor-market transition, and scenario planning rather than through a single fixed industrial-policy blueprint.

## What this source adds
- Anthropic emphasizes uncertainty about the rate, scale, and form of AI-driven economic disruption.
- It uses observed product usage trends, especially the shift from collaboration toward delegation, as an empirical signal that labor impacts may deepen.
- It presents policy ideas as scenario-dependent starting points for debate rather than as a finalized company platform.

## Themes in this source
- upskilling and workforce training
- support for displaced workers
- compute and energy permitting reform
- possible taxes or public revenue mechanisms if disruption intensifies
- broad consultation with economists and policy experts

## Claude Code as product strategy
- Anthropic is using claude-code to argue that software development is shifting toward orchestration of agents rather than manual code writing alone.
- Its public materials emphasize verification, approvals, and human judgment as central operating principles for agentic coding.
- This makes Anthropic’s agentic product stance somewhat more operationally disciplined and coding-centered than the broader workspace framing in codex.

## Relationships
- Anthropic’s framing contrasts with the more declarative agenda in industrial-policy-for-the-intelligence-age.
- Its scenario logic informs anthropic-google-openai-us-government-ai-policy-framings.

## Open questions
- How should uncertainty-sensitive policy planning be converted into concrete legislative action?
- To what extent does Anthropic’s labor framing differ in practice from OpenAI’s and Google’s more opportunity-led narratives?

title: Claude Code
summary: claude-code is anthropic’s agentic coding system. Anthropic frames it as a project-level software agent that can read codebases, modify multiple files, run commands and tests, and deliver code ready for review rather tha
text:
# Claude Code

## Summary
claude-code is anthropic’s agentic coding system. Anthropic frames it as a project-level software agent that can read codebases, modify multiple files, run commands and tests, and deliver code ready for review rather than merely suggesting snippets.

## Product framing
- It is presented as an agentic coding environment rather than a chat-style assistant.
- Anthropic explicitly argues software development is shifting from writing code to orchestrating agents that write code.
- The product is also framed as usable by non-engineers who can clearly describe goals.

## Operating philosophy
- Human oversight remains central through approvals and review.
- Good verification loops are treated as essential: tests, screenshots, expected outputs.
- Anthropic highlights practical constraints such as context-window management and the need for scoped prompts.

## Strategic significance
claude-code appears to represent Anthropic’s preferred path into agentic-software-development: a high-trust agent embedded in real codebases and toolchains, with strong emphasis on iterative verification and human judgment.

## Related pages
- anthropic is the company advancing this product.
- codex is a major competing product in the same category.
- agentic-software-development is the broader pattern both products illustrate.

title: Codex
summary: codex is openai’s agentic software-development product line. Across the 2025–2026 releases, it evolves from a cloud coding agent into a broader technical-work agent that can operate computers, use tools and plugins, pres
text:
# Codex

## Summary
codex is openai’s agentic software-development product line. Across the 2025–2026 releases, it evolves from a cloud coding agent into a broader technical-work agent that can operate computers, use tools and plugins, preserve context across time, and support long-running parallel workflows.

## Release arc
- May 2025: Codex launches as a cloud-based software engineering agent inside ChatGPT, focused on repository-grounded coding tasks in isolated sandboxes.
- February 2026: openai introduces GPT-5.3-Codex and explicitly frames Codex as a step toward a single general-purpose agent for technical work, not just code generation.
- April 2026: the “Codex for (almost) everything” update adds computer use, browser-native workflows, image generation, plugins/MCP, memory, automations, and more lifecycle coverage.

## Why this matters
- Codex is a concrete example of agentic-software-development moving from point assistance to delegated execution.
- The product direction suggests that software work is being reorganized around supervision of multiple asynchronous agents rather than one-shot autocomplete.
- It also shows how frontier AI products increasingly connect model capability, tools, memory, and workflow orchestration in one system.

## Notable capabilities across the releases
- Parallel task execution in isolated environments
- Verifiable logs and test outputs for review
- Interactive steering while the agent is still working
- Computer use across GUI applications
- In-app browser feedback loops for frontend and app iteration
- Plugins, skills, and MCP-based integration with external tools
- Scheduled automations and persistent context across repeated tasks

## Strategic interpretation
openai is using Codex to operationalize its broader arguments about advanced AI: the future is not just better models, but systems that can reason, act, coordinate across tools, and remain reviewable by humans. In that sense, Codex is an applied product bridge between current software engineering and longer-run ideas about superintelligence-governance.

## Competitive context
- codex is best understood relative to claude-code on the proprietary side and hermes-agent / openclaw on the open-platform side.
- Its distinctive move is turning agentic coding into a broader hosted technical-work workspace.

## Open questions
- Which parts of the workflow remain bottlenecked on human trust, review, and approval?
- Does broad computer use make Codex more useful faster than it makes safety and permissions harder?
- How much of the long-run value comes from the model versus the surrounding agent platform?

title: Google
summary: Google frames AI policy around a three-part agenda of opportunity, responsibility, and security, emphasizing multi-stakeholder governance, international coordination, and continued innovation rather than halting developm
text:
# Google

## Summary
Google frames AI policy around a three-part agenda of opportunity, responsibility, and security, emphasizing multi-stakeholder governance, international coordination, and continued innovation rather than halting development.

## What this source adds
- Google explicitly argues against broad pauses or halts in AI progress.
- It presents governance as a combination of private-sector practices, industry standards, government policy, and international coordination.
- It highlights public trust, economic competitiveness, workforce transition, and geopolitical security as simultaneous policy goals.

## Themes in this source
- innovation and competitiveness
- responsible governance and risk reduction
- international coordination
- privacy and cross-border data flows
- workforce upskilling and future-of-work research

## Relationships
- Google’s three-pillar framing resembles parts of industrial-policy-for-the-intelligence-age but is less centered on distributional industrial policy.
- Its priorities are compared directly in anthropic-google-openai-us-government-ai-policy-framings.

## Open questions
- How does Google’s multi-stakeholder governance framing interact with its commercial incentives and global platform position?
- Which concrete enforcement mechanisms follow from the responsibility pillar beyond high-level governance language?

title: Hermes Agent
summary: hermes-agent is an open-source agent framework by Nous Research focused on persistent memory, reusable skills, multi-platform access, and provider flexibility. Unlike narrowly hosted coding agents, it is designed as a ge
text:
# Hermes Agent

## Summary
hermes-agent is an open-source agent framework by Nous Research focused on persistent memory, reusable skills, multi-platform access, and provider flexibility. Unlike narrowly hosted coding agents, it is designed as a general-purpose agent that can live across terminal, messaging platforms, and tool ecosystems.

## Differentiators
- Persistent memory and durable user/environment context
- Self-improving skills that accumulate procedures over time
- Multi-platform messaging gateways in addition to terminal and local tooling
- Provider-agnostic model routing rather than single-vendor lock-in
- Extensibility through plugins, MCP, cron, and custom tools

## Release direction
The release arc through v0.10.0 suggests rapid expansion in tooling, gateway distribution, safety controls, profiles, dashboards, and managed tool access. This points toward an open agent platform strategy rather than a pure coding-workspace strategy.

## Why it matters
hermes-agent is relevant to agentic-software-development because it shows an alternative design center: not just “best coding agent,” but “best persistent agent shell” that can support coding, research, automation, operations, and messaging-native workflows.

## Related pages
- codex represents a hosted, vendor-integrated alternative.
- claude-code represents a tightly focused agentic coding environment.
- openclaw is another open, user-controlled agent direction.

title: OpenAI
summary: OpenAI is a frontier AI lab/company that, in this source, argues the transition toward superintelligence is already underway and should be shaped by democratic, people-first industrial policy rather than by market concen
text:
# OpenAI

## Summary
OpenAI is a frontier AI lab/company that, in this source, argues the transition toward superintelligence is already underway and should be shaped by democratic, people-first industrial policy rather than by market concentration or purely technical governance alone.

## What this source adds
- OpenAI frames AI governance as both an economic and democratic design problem, not only a safety-engineering problem.
- The paper presents a three-part policy objective: share prosperity broadly, mitigate risks, and democratize access and agency.
- OpenAI proposes practical policy engagement steps including fellowships, research grants, API-credit support, and convenings in Washington, DC.

## Positions and themes
- OpenAI explicitly links frontier capability growth to the need for public-private collaboration.
- It argues AI data centers should bear their own energy costs and produce local jobs and tax revenue.
- It warns against both regulatory capture and centralized control.
- It positions itself as an actor seeking to influence U.S.-centric but globally relevant AI industrial policy.


## Codex as product strategy
- The Codex release sequence shows openai pushing toward agentic execution rather than only chat or code generation.
- In 2025 Codex is positioned as an asynchronous cloud coding agent; by April 2026 it is framed as a broader technical-work platform with computer use, browser workflows, integrations, memory, and automations.
- This strengthens the interpretation that OpenAI’s product strategy is converging around agents that can reason, act, and remain steerable inside real workflows.

## Relationships
- This policy paper is central to industrial-policy-for-the-intelligence-age.
- Its governance framing depends on claims about superintelligence-governance.

## Competitive context
- codex now sits in a direct product category contest with claude-code and with open agent frameworks such as hermes-agent.

## Open questions
- How much of OpenAI’s industrial-policy framing is best understood as public-interest policy versus strategic positioning by a frontier lab?
- How should OpenAI’s policy asks be compared against proposals from other labs, governments, and civil-society organizations?

title: OpenClaw
summary: openclaw is an open-source personal AI assistant positioned around user-controlled infrastructure, persistent memory, chat-app access, and full-system/browser control on the user’s own machine.
text:
# OpenClaw

## Summary
openclaw is an open-source personal AI assistant positioned around user-controlled infrastructure, persistent memory, chat-app access, and full-system/browser control on the user’s own machine.

## Product framing
- Marketed as personal rather than enterprise-first.
- Emphasizes on-prem or user-controlled execution rather than a hosted workspace.
- Supports a wide range of chat surfaces and model backends.

## Strategic significance
openclaw is part of the open-agent wave around agentic-software-development. It suggests that some of the future of agentic AI may be controlled by users through hackable personal infrastructure, not only by frontier vendors through vertically integrated SaaS workspaces.

## Related pages
- hermes-agent shares the open, extensible, multi-surface direction.
- codex and claude-code illustrate more vertically integrated frontier-lab approaches.

title: United States Government
summary: In this source, the U.S. government frames AI policy as a strategic contest for national power, with emphasis on deregulation, infrastructure buildout, energy supply, export controls, and international AI leadership.
text:
# United States Government

## Summary
In this source, the U.S. government frames AI policy as a strategic contest for national power, with emphasis on deregulation, infrastructure buildout, energy supply, export controls, and international AI leadership.

## What this source adds
- AI is treated as an economic and military competition in which ecosystem scale matters.
- The policy center of gravity is national competitiveness and dominance rather than broad democratic co-design.
- Infrastructure, chips, data centers, and power generation are elevated to first-order policy priorities.

## Themes in this source
- deregulation to accelerate AI innovation
- infrastructure and energy expansion
- federal procurement leverage
- ideological neutrality framing
- diplomacy, export control, and geopolitical competition

## Relationships
- The action plan is a major input into anthropic-google-openai-us-government-ai-policy-framings.
- Its buildout logic overlaps partially with industrial-policy-for-the-intelligence-age while differing sharply in rhetoric and emphasis.

## Open questions
- How durable is this framework across administrations or congressional priorities?
- Which parts of the plan are best understood as industrial policy, national-security policy, or culture-war procurement policy?

title: Agentic Software Development
summary: agentic-software-development is a development model in which humans supervise software agents that can plan, execute, test, use tools, and continue work asynchronously. In the Codex materials, this model shifts the devel
text:
# Agentic Software Development

## Summary
agentic-software-development is a development model in which humans supervise software agents that can plan, execute, test, use tools, and continue work asynchronously. In the Codex materials, this model shifts the developer role from directly performing every edit to delegating scoped work, steering execution, and reviewing evidence-backed outputs.

## Core characteristics
- Asynchronous execution: agents keep working after the prompt is sent.
- Parallelism: multiple tasks or threads can run at once.
- Grounded environments: work happens inside sandboxes, repositories, browsers, terminals, or full computer-use interfaces.
- Reviewability: logs, diffs, test results, and artifacts help humans audit the work.
- Interactive steering: the human can redirect the agent mid-task without fully restarting.
- Continuity: memory, saved preferences, and automations make work persistent over days or weeks.

## Why Codex is a useful case study
codex shows the progression from coding assistant to workflow agent:
- first by taking on bounded repo tasks,
- then by combining coding and broader reasoning,
- then by integrating browsers, GUIs, remote machines, plugins, and scheduled automations.

## Broader implications for agentic AI
- Agentic systems become more valuable when they combine model capability with tool access, state, and organizational workflow hooks.
- The relevant competitive frontier shifts from “best code completion” toward “best delegated execution system.”
- Product design matters as much as raw model IQ: permissions, handoff, task tracking, memory, and evidence become core infrastructure.

## Risks and limits
- More autonomy increases the importance of secure execution, approval boundaries, and auditability.
- Benchmark gains do not automatically solve deployment issues such as ambiguous requirements, weak tests, or brittle environment setup.
- Wider computer use raises the stakes for misuse, security review, and policy controls.

## Related pages
- codex is a primary example of this pattern.
- openai is one of the main companies explicitly pushing this framing.
- superintelligence-governance matters because growing agent competence changes what institutions must govern, not just what models can say.

title: Industrial Policy for the Intelligence Age
summary: A policy framing, advanced by openai, that argues frontier AI and the move toward superintelligence require an updated industrial policy to distribute gains broadly, scale safety with capability, and preserve democratic
text:
# Industrial Policy for the Intelligence Age

## Summary
A policy framing, advanced by openai, that argues frontier AI and the move toward superintelligence require an updated industrial policy to distribute gains broadly, scale safety with capability, and preserve democratic agency.

## Core claims from the source
- AI is moving from automating tasks that take minutes to tasks that take hours and potentially months.
- Market forces alone may not distribute benefits or respond fast enough to major capability transitions.
- Industrial policy should combine research funding, workforce development, regulation, procurement, and investment.
- Public-private experimentation and measurement should complement formal regulation.

## Stated goals
1. Share prosperity broadly
2. Mitigate risks
3. Democratize access and agency

## Specific policy directions mentioned
- Formal worker voice in AI deployment decisions
- Energy and local-tax accountability for AI data centers
- Institutional mechanisms that avoid regulatory capture
- Pilots, grants, and public-facing policy iteration

## Why it matters for the wiki
This is a useful umbrella page for AI political economy, compute infrastructure externalities, and labor/governance questions that sit adjacent to purely technical AI research.

## Related pages
- openai is the organization articulating this policy document.
- superintelligence-governance covers the capability-governance premise behind the policy argument.

## Open questions
- Which parts of this agenda are unique to AI versus reapplications of classic industrial-policy tools?
- What metrics would show whether prosperity, agency, and risk mitigation are actually being shared rather than merely promised?

title: Superintelligence Governance
summary: A governance framing in which policymakers and institutions prepare for AI systems that could outperform the smartest humans even when those humans are assisted by AI. In this source, openai treats that transition as alr
text:
# Superintelligence Governance

## Summary
A governance framing in which policymakers and institutions prepare for AI systems that could outperform the smartest humans even when those humans are assisted by AI. In this source, openai treats that transition as already beginning and argues that safety, distribution, and democratic legitimacy must scale alongside capability.

## Claims in this source
- The transition to superintelligence is framed as underway rather than hypothetical.
- Safety must scale with capability.
- Risks include misuse, loss of alignment or control, anti-democratic use, and concentration of wealth and power.
- Governance is not only about preventing catastrophe; it is also about who gets agency, access, and economic upside.

## Policy implications surfaced here
- Governance should address labor, institutional adaptation, and wealth concentration, not just model misuse.
- Democratic processes should have real influence over how advanced AI is deployed.
- Industrial policy may become part of frontier-model governance, especially where compute, energy, and regional development are involved.

## Related pages
- industrial-policy-for-the-intelligence-age is one concrete policy response built on this framing.
- openai is the actor advancing this particular argument in the source.

## Open questions
- What thresholds meaningfully distinguish frontier AI from superintelligence in governance practice?
- Which governance mechanisms remain robust if capability progress is slower or less discontinuous than this paper assumes?

title: Anthropic, Google, OpenAI, and U.S. Government AI Policy Framings
summary: These four approaches overlap on the importance of AI, infrastructure, and state involvement, but they differ sharply on what problem AI policy is primarily trying to solve. openai emphasizes democratic distribution and
text:
# Anthropic, Google, OpenAI, and U.S. Government AI Policy Framings

## Summary
These four approaches overlap on the importance of AI, infrastructure, and state involvement, but they differ sharply on what problem AI policy is primarily trying to solve. openai emphasizes democratic distribution and social legitimacy, anthropic emphasizes uncertainty and scenario planning for labor disruption, google emphasizes innovation plus responsible multi-stakeholder governance, and the united-states-government source emphasizes strategic dominance, deregulation, and national power.

## Comparison table

| Actor | Core framing | Main policy goal | Dominant risks emphasized | Preferred policy style | Economic distribution emphasis | Infrastructure emphasis | Security / geopolitics emphasis |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| openai | Industrial policy for the intelligence age | Share prosperity, mitigate risks, democratize access and agency | concentration of wealth/power, misuse, loss of control, anti-democratic use | public-private industrial policy with democratic legitimacy | high | medium-high | medium |
| anthropic | Uncertain economic transition requiring scenario-dependent responses | Prepare early for a range of labor-market outcomes | displacement, wage pressure, unclear disruption pathways | scenario planning, economic-policy options, expert consultation | medium-high | medium | low-medium |
| google | Responsible AI progress through opportunity, responsibility, security | Preserve innovation while governing misuse and sustaining trust | misinformation, discrimination, misuse, trust failures, geopolitical risks | multi-stakeholder governance, standards, international coordination | medium | medium | medium-high |
| united-states-government | Strategic race for global AI dominance | Accelerate innovation, build infrastructure, lead globally | losing technological leadership, adversary advantage, infrastructure bottlenecks | deregulation, procurement leverage, export controls, state-backed buildout | low-medium | very high | very high |

## Key similarities
- All four sources reject a passive wait-and-see approach.
- All four assume AI will be economically and politically transformative.
- All four see infrastructure and/or institutional adaptation as necessary.
- None of the sources argue for simply freezing AI progress.

## Key differences

### 1. What is the primary policy problem?
- openai: the main problem is distributing the gains and governance power of superintelligence without allowing concentration or democratic erosion.
- anthropic: the main problem is uncertainty about labor and economic disruption, requiring policies that are conditional on how disruption unfolds.
- google: the main problem is enabling beneficial innovation while reducing harms and maintaining trust.
- united-states-government: the main problem is winning a strategic race and preventing national decline or adversary advantage.

### 2. How central is economic redistribution?
- openai puts distribution and agency near the center.
- anthropic is open to redistribution-like tools, including worker support and possible new revenue systems, but presents them conditionally.
- google discusses workforce transition and broad benefits, but distribution is not the center of its framing.
- united-states-government emphasizes jobs and productivity rhetorically, but the plan is more focused on scale, infrastructure, and competitive position than on redistribution.

### 3. How is state capacity imagined?
- openai imagines the state as a democratic partner shaping prosperity, safety, and access.
- anthropic imagines the state as a planner and stabilizer under uncertainty.
- google imagines the state as one participant in a broader multi-stakeholder governance ecosystem.
- united-states-government imagines the state as a strategic accelerator, market shaper, and geopolitical coordinator.

### 4. What role does infrastructure play?
- openai treats data-center energy accountability and local economic effects as part of legitimate AI industrial policy.
- anthropic discusses permitting and infrastructure mainly as enabling constraints that matter across scenarios.
- google discusses infrastructure less centrally in this source than innovation frameworks and governance.
- united-states-government makes infrastructure a first-order national mission spanning chips, data centers, and power generation.

## Synthesis
The four framings can be understood as answering different first questions:
- openai asks: how do we keep superintelligence people-first?
- anthropic asks: how do we prepare if labor disruption accelerates faster than expected?
- google asks: how do we keep innovation moving while making governance credible enough for adoption and trust?
- united-states-government asks: how does America win the AI race?

This means apparent disagreement is partly about policy substance and partly about which objective is treated as primary. OpenAI and Anthropic are closer on labor and social-transition concerns than Google or the U.S. government source. Google and the U.S. government source are closer on competitiveness and continued deployment, though Google’s framing is more multi-stakeholder and less overtly nationalist. OpenAI and the U.S. government source both invoke industrial-policy-like instruments, but they diverge strongly on normative center of gravity: democratic distribution versus strategic dominance.

## Practical use for the wiki
This comparison is a useful anchor page for future policy-source ingestion. New sources can be slotted into these axes:
- distribution vs dominance
- uncertainty-management vs declared doctrine
- multi-stakeholder governance vs state-led competition
- labor transition vs infrastructure buildout

## Related pages
- industrial-policy-for-the-intelligence-age captures the OpenAI source in more detail.
- superintelligence-governance covers the capability-governance premise beneath OpenAI’s framing.

title: Codex vs Claude Code and Open Agent Platforms
summary: codex and claude-code are converging on a similar thesis: software development is moving from hand-coding toward supervision of agents that can plan, execute, test, and iterate. But they differ in product center of gravi
text:
# Codex vs Claude Code and Open Agent Platforms

## Summary
codex and claude-code are converging on a similar thesis: software development is moving from hand-coding toward supervision of agents that can plan, execute, test, and iterate. But they differ in product center of gravity. codex is evolving into a broad, hosted technical-work workspace with computer use, automations, plugins, and multimodal orchestration. claude-code is more tightly framed around deeply agentic coding inside real codebases and toolchains, with strong emphasis on verification and human judgment. Open frameworks like hermes-agent and openclaw push a different thesis again: the future may also belong to user-controlled, extensible, cross-surface agents rather than only frontier-lab SaaS workspaces.

## Quick comparison table

| System | Primary identity | Design center | Strength of current framing | Control model | Notable tradeoff |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| codex | Hosted agentic technical-work platform | Full lifecycle orchestration, computer use, plugins, automations | Broadest workspace vision | Vendor-integrated, managed product | More action surface means more governance and permissions complexity |
| claude-code | Agentic coding system | Codebase-level execution, testing, CI/toolchain use, verification | Sharpest coding-specific operating model | Hosted/proprietary but human-approval-centric | Narrower public framing beyond coding than Codex |
| hermes-agent | Open agent framework | Persistent memory, skills, gateways, provider flexibility, extensibility | Broadest open general-agent shell | User-controlled and hackable | More assembly/orchestration burden on the user |
| openclaw | Open personal agent | Personal infrastructure, chat-native control, system/browser access | Strongest personal-agent framing | User-controlled and on-prem leaning | Less obviously enterprise-standardized than frontier-lab products |

## Codex vs Claude Code

### Where they agree
- Both are explicit examples of agentic-software-development.
- Both move beyond autocomplete into delegated multi-step execution.
- Both assume the developer increasingly becomes a supervisor, reviewer, and orchestrator.
- Both emphasize codebase/tool grounding rather than pure chat abstraction.

### Where Codex is stronger in public positioning
- Broader claim to become a general technical-work agent, not just a coding agent.
- More explicit movement into computer use, in-app browsing, image generation, remote machines, and scheduled automations.
- Stronger “workspace/control-plane” framing for parallel agents.

### Where Claude Code is stronger in public positioning
- Clearer articulation of the operational discipline needed for agentic coding to work in practice.
- More emphasis on tests, expected outputs, screenshots, scoped prompts, and approval boundaries.
- Stronger rhetoric around organization-wide coding adoption and shifts in engineering roles.

### Interpretation
If Codex looks like an agent operating environment, Claude Code looks like a high-discipline coding copilot upgraded into a codebase operator. Codex is expanding outward from coding into adjacent work; Claude Code is digging deeper into coding itself while still pointing toward multi-agent orchestration.

## Section on OpenClaw and Hermes Agent
Assumption: this section interprets “open law” as openclaw, which appears to fit the agentic-AI context. If that is not what you meant, this section should be revised.

### What open platforms change
hermes-agent and openclaw matter because they represent a different competitive logic from codex and claude-code:
- the agent lives closer to the user,
- context and memory are user-controlled,
- the system can span chat apps, terminal, home automation, web, and custom tools,
- model choice can be swapped underneath the same workflow shell.

### Hermes Agent specifically
hermes-agent pushes the idea that the durable advantage in agentic AI may come from:
- persistent memory,
- reusable skills,
- multi-platform reach,
- provider portability, and
- extensibility.

This is strategically important because it treats agent quality as a compounding system property, not just a model property. In that sense, Hermes is closer to an agent runtime/framework than a single polished product surface.

### OpenClaw specifically
openclaw highlights a personal-agent thesis: users may want an agent that sits on their own machine, inside their own communication channels, with direct control over files, browser flows, and day-to-day automations. This is a different route into agentic AI than enterprise coding productivity.

### Strategic takeaway
Open systems like hermes-agent and openclaw imply that the future agent market may split three ways:
1. Hosted frontier-lab workspaces like codex
2. Focused proprietary coding operators like claude-code
3. Open user-controlled agent runtimes like hermes-agent and openclaw

## What this means for the future
- The contest is no longer just about who has the best model; it is about who has the best agent architecture.
- Memory, permissions, orchestration, verification, integrations, and workflow continuity are becoming core product features.
- Open and closed ecosystems may diverge in values: convenience and polish versus control and extensibility.
- Multi-agent supervision is becoming a standard pattern across both proprietary and open systems.

## Synthesis
codex and claude-code both validate the move to agentic software development, but they optimize for somewhat different futures. Codex suggests a broad, multimodal, hosted agent workspace. Claude Code suggests a deeply practical execution engine for code work under human supervision. hermes-agent and openclaw widen the frame further: the long-run winner may be not just the smartest coding agent, but the most adaptable agent environment around the user.

## Follow-on comparison
- codex-vs-claude-code-vs-hermes-agent-architecture-trust-moats drills into architecture, trust,

title: Codex vs Claude Code vs Hermes Agent on Architecture, Trust, and Moats
summary: codex, claude-code, and hermes-agent all point toward the same future: users increasingly supervise agents instead of directly performing every technical step. But their underlying bets differ. codex is betting on a mana
text:
# Codex vs Claude Code vs Hermes Agent on Architecture, Trust, and Moats

## Summary
codex, claude-code, and hermes-agent all point toward the same future: users increasingly supervise agents instead of directly performing every technical step. But their underlying bets differ. codex is betting on a managed, vertically integrated agent workspace. claude-code is betting on a highly capable coding operator whose value comes from strong execution discipline and verification inside real software workflows. hermes-agent is betting on an open, persistent, user-controlled runtime where memory, skills, tools, and provider choice compound over time.

## Architecture comparison

| System | Core architectural pattern | Where it lives | State model | Tool model | Operating style |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| codex | Hosted agent workspace | OpenAI-managed product surfaces | Persistent threads, automations, memory, app-level coordination | Plugins, MCP, browser, terminals, computer use, remote machines | Broad technical-work orchestration |
| claude-code | Agentic coding environment | Anthropic-managed coding environment | Session/context-centered with strong prompt/process discipline | Codebase access, shell/toolchain/CI use, testing, approvals | Deep codebase execution under supervision |
| hermes-agent | Open agent runtime/framework | User terminal, messaging platforms, local/remote environments | Persistent memory, skills, profiles, sessions, cron, multi-platform history | Pluggable toolsets, MCP, browser, terminal, file, delegation, messaging gateways | General-purpose persistent agent shell |

## Architecture interpretation
- codex is architected like a control plane for hosted agents.
- claude-code is architected like a coding operator embedded in a practical development loop.
- hermes-agent is architected like an agent runtime that can be shaped around the user’s environment.

The strategic difference is important: Codex and Claude Code concentrate value in the managed product; Hermes concentrates value in the extensible shell plus accumulated user context.

## Trust model comparison

| System | Main trust mechanism | Human role | Main risk boundary | Trust challenge |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| codex | Reviewability plus product-managed permissions | Supervisor of parallel tasks and outputs | Hosted permissions, automation scope, computer-use boundaries | As action surface expands, users must trust a broader managed system |
| claude-code | Verification discipline: tests, screenshots, expected outputs, approvals | Specifier, reviewer, and approver | Code, commands, CI, and environment actions | Strong results depend on disciplined user inputs and verification loops |
| hermes-agent | User control, transparency, profile/tool restrictions, memory/skill ownership | Operator, configurator, and ongoing trainer | Local tools, connected services, platform access, custom integrations | Power and flexibility increase configuration burden and safety responsibility |

## Trust interpretation
claude-code has the clearest public philosophy of earned trust through verification. codex is broader and more productized, which may make it more convenient but also increases the importance of strong permissions and audit design. hermes-agent offers the highest degree of user control, but that also means the user or organization carries more of the operational safety burden.

## Moat comparison

| System | Likely moat | Why it matters | Possible weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| codex | Vertically integrated workspace, model access, product surface breadth, enterprise workflow capture | The more work flows through one managed agent workspace, the harder it is to switch | Can be challenged if open standards reduce lock-in or if users prefer more control |
| claude-code | Execution quality in coding workflows plus trusted operating discipline | If it consistently delivers better code changes with fewer failures, teams may standardize on it | More coding-centric framing may narrow perceived surface area versus broader agent platforms |
| hermes-agent | User-owned memory, skills, profiles, multi-platform presence, provider portability, extensibility | Long-term value compounds in the user’s environment rather than inside one vendor product | Harder to package, support, and standardize than a polished hosted SaaS experience |

## What each system is really optimizing for
- codex optimizes for breadth of delegated technical work.
- claude-code optimizes for depth and reliability of coding execution.
- hermes-agent optimizes for durability, portability, and user-controlled compounding context.

## Strategic synthesis
These are not just three products; they are three different theories of agent advantage:
1. Managed orchestration advantage — codex
2. Execution-discipline advantage — claude-code
3. Persistent user-context advantage — hermes-agent

A likely future is that organizations use more than one model at once: a managed frontier agent for some tasks, a specialized coding operator for others, and an open persistent runtime for workflows where control, memory ownership, or custom integrations matter more.

## Related pages
- codex-vs-claude-code-and-open-agent-platforms
- codex
- claude-code
- hermes-agent
- agentic-software-development

title: OpenAI Codex, agentic AI, and what the latest release implies
summary: The latest major Codex release is “Codex for (almost) everything” from 2026-04-16. In context, it suggests that openai no longer sees Codex merely as a coding model or IDE helper. It is being turned into a general techni
text:
# OpenAI Codex, agentic AI, and what the latest release implies

## Bottom line
The latest major Codex release is “Codex for (almost) everything” from 2026-04-16. In context, it suggests that openai no longer sees Codex merely as a coding model or IDE helper. It is being turned into a general technical-work agent platform: able to use computers, browsers, plugins, memory, automations, and multiple concurrent threads.

## What changed in the latest release
Compared with the 2025 launch and the February 2026 GPT-5.3-Codex release, the April 2026 update pushes Codex in five important directions:
1. From code sandbox to full computer use — the agent can see, click, and type in GUI apps.
2. From coding to broader tool orchestration — plugins, skills, and MCP integrations widen the action surface.
3. From single task to ongoing work — automations and persistent thread context support repeated tasks over time.
4. From text/code only to multimodal work — browser-native review and image generation enter the workflow.
5. From isolated assistance to workspace control plane — Codex becomes a supervisory environment for parallel agents, terminals, review flows, and remote machines.

## Why this matters for agentic AI
codex makes a broader point about agentic-software-development and agentic AI more generally:
- The winning products are likely to be systems, not just models.
- The practical unit of progress is shifting from “answer quality” to delegated work completed under supervision.
- Agent value increasingly comes from combining reasoning with tools, memory, permissions, context retention, and execution environments.
- Human work shifts upward from direct manipulation toward task framing, review, exception handling, and approval.

## What this may mean for the future

### 1. Agents will spread from coding into adjacent knowledge work
OpenAI’s own framing already moves from coding into docs, slides, spreadsheets, browser tasks, PR review, research, and repeatable operations. That implies convergence between coding agents and general workplace agents.

### 2. “Computer use” becomes strategically important
Once an agent can operate software that lacks APIs, the reachable task surface expands dramatically. This is a major unlock for agentic AI, though it also increases safety, permissions, and monitoring challenges.

### 3. The interface layer becomes a moat
Model quality still matters, but the real product moat may be the surrounding workspace: approvals, thread continuity, artifact tracking, integrations, review UX, and automation reliability.

### 4. Multi-agent supervision becomes a normal mode of work
The strongest claim in the Codex arc is not that one agent becomes perfect, but that humans can manage several imperfect but useful agents in parallel. That could restructure software-team workflows before fully autonomous agents arrive.

### 5. Governance pressure rises with usefulness
OpenAI’s February release already linked GPT-5.3-Codex to higher cyber capability. The more agentic systems can browse, execute, and persist over time, the more governance has to focus on actionability, not just generated text. That connects directly to broader questions in superintelligence-governance.

## Comparison context
Compared with claude-code, codex is currently framed more expansively as a general technical-work workspace. Compared with hermes-agent and openclaw, Codex is more vertically integrated and vendor-managed, but less user-controlled and hackable.

## My synthesis
Codex looks less like “better Copilot” and more like an early operating system for applied agents. The future implied by these releases is one where:
- coding is a beachhead, not the endpoint;
- agents become long-running coworkers rather than one-turn assistants;
- reviewability and permissions matter as much as raw intelligence;
- companies compete on orchestration, trust, and integration, not only on benchmark scores.

## Useful caution
The future is not guaranteed to arrive smoothly. Agentic systems still depend on good task specification, good tests, secure environments, and human willingness to trust them. So the near-term effect is likely workflow reorganization before full labor replacement.

## Related pages
- codex
- agentic-software-development
- openai
- superintelligence-governance

title: # Wiki Schema
summary: AI research and adjacent AI engineering knowledge: models, labs, techniques, benchmarks, tooling, evaluation, inference, training, productization, and important people/organizations. The wiki is intended to compound over
text:
# Wiki Schema

## Domain
AI research and adjacent AI engineering knowledge: models, labs, techniques, benchmarks, tooling, evaluation, inference, training, productization, and important people/organizations. The wiki is intended to compound over time into a practical research and operations knowledge base, not a raw clipping archive.

## Conventions
- File names: lowercase, hyphens, no spaces (e.g., transformer-architecture.md)
- Every wiki page starts with YAML frontmatter
- Use wikilinks to link between pages (minimum 2 outbound links per page)
- When updating a page, always bump the updated date
- Every new page must be added to index.md under the correct section
- Every action must be appended to log.md
- Raw source files live under raw/ and are immutable after capture
- Prefer concise, scannable pages; move long derivations or deep dives into separate pages
- Use direct source-based claims; if a claim is uncertain, label it explicitly

## Frontmatter
 

## Tag Taxonomy
- model
- architecture
- benchmark
- training
- inference
- evaluation
- dataset
- alignment
- safety
- agent
- reasoning
- multimodal
- vision
- speech
- robotics
- company
- lab
- person
- open-source
- product
- tooling
- infrastructure
- gpu
- efficiency
- fine-tuning
- reinforcement-learning
- synthetic-data
- regulation
- security
- timeline
- comparison
- controversy
- prediction

Rule: every tag on a page must appear in this taxonomy. If a new tag is needed, add it here first, then use it.

## Page Thresholds
- Create a page when an entity/concept appears in 2+ sources OR is central to one source
- Add to an existing page when a source mentions something already covered
- Don't create a page for passing mentions, minor details, or things outside AI research scope
- Split a page when it exceeds ~200 lines; break into sub-topics with cross-links
- Archive a page when its content is fully superseded; move to _archive/, remove from index

## Entity Pages
One page per notable entity. Include:
- Overview / what it is
- Key facts and dates
- Relationships to other entities (wikilinks)
- Source references

Entity categories commonly include people, companies, labs, open-source projects, products, models, and benchmarks when a dedicated entity page is more natural than a concept page.

## Concept Pages
One page per concept or topic. Include:
- Definition / explanation
- Current state of knowledge
- Open questions or debates
- Related concepts (wikilinks)

Common concept topics include training methods, evaluation patterns, inference systems, scaling laws, agent workflows, safety methods, and benchmark design.

## Comparison Pages
Side-by-side analyses. Include:
- What is being compared and why
- Dimensions of comparison (table format preferred)
- Verdict or synthesis
- Sources

Examples: model-vs-model, benchmark-vs-benchmark, lab strategy comparisons, inference stack trade-offs.

## Query Pages
Use queries/ for substantial answers worth preserving, such as:
- deep research syntheses
- recurring user questions
- multi-source comparisons that would be expensive to reconstruct

Do not file trivial lookups.

## Update Policy
When new information conflicts with existing content:
1. Check dates — newer sources generally supersede older ones
2. If genuinely contradictory, note both positions with dates and sources
3. Mark the contradiction in frontmatter: contradictions: [page-name]
4. Flag for user review in the lint report

## Default Structure Hints
- entities/ for labs, companies, products, notable researchers, named models, datasets, benchmarks
- concepts/ for methods, abstractions, research themes, technical mechanisms
- comparisons/ for structured side-by-side analyses
- queries/ for reusable syntheses and non-trivial question answers

## Preferred Style
- Lead with a compact summary section
- Prefer bullets and short sections over long prose
- Keep claims attributable to sources
- Include open questions where the field is unsettled
- Link outward aggressively so the wiki becomes navigable as it grows

# Best Practices for Claude Code — Summary

Source: https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-best-practices
Captured: 2026-04-19
Type: web article

## Core thesis
Claude Code is an agentic coding environment where the user describes goals and the agent explores, plans, implements, and verifies.

## Key points
- Performance depends heavily on good verification loops: tests, screenshots, expected outputs.
- Anthropic recommends a workflow of explore → plan → implement → commit.
- Specific context and scoping materially improve performance.
- Context-window limits are a practical constraint in long agentic sessions.

## Key excerpts
> “Claude Code is an agentic coding environment, not just a chat assistant.”

> “Include tests, screenshots, or expected outputs so Claude can check itself.”

# Claude Code by Anthropic — Summary

Source: https://www.anthropic.com/product/claude-code
Captured: 2026-04-19
Type: web article

## Core proposition
Claude Code is Anthropic’s agentic coding system that reads a codebase, makes changes across files, runs tests, and delivers committed code.

## Key points
- Positioned as a project-level coding agent, not autocomplete.
- Anthropic says the majority of code at Anthropic is now written by Claude Code.
- Emphasizes delegation of full tasks, multi-file changes, tool use, CI monitoring, and iterative debugging.
- Also framed as usable by non-engineers who can specify goals clearly.
- Safety model emphasizes default caution, human approvals, and adjustable autonomy.

## Key excerpts
> “Claude Code is an agentic coding system that reads your codebase, makes changes across files, runs tests, and delivers committed code.”

> “At Anthropic, the majority of code is now written by Claude Code.”

title: # Preparing for AI’s economic impact: exploring policy responses
summary: Source URL: https://www.anthropic.com/research/economic-policy-responses
text:
# Preparing for AI’s economic impact: exploring policy responses

Source URL: https://www.anthropic.com/research/economic-policy-responses
Publisher: Anthropic
Date: 2025-10-14
Captured: 2026-04-19
Type: Web article

## Extracted notes

## Core thesis

Anthropic argues that AI’s economic effects are highly uncertain, but policymakers should prepare now for multiple scenarios. The company highlights policy categories spanning workforce development, tax and fiscal policy, infrastructure and permitting reform, worker support, and broader social revenue systems.

A major motivation is Anthropic’s observation that usage is shifting from human-AI collaboration toward delegation of complete tasks to AI, which may increase labor-market disruption as models become more independently capable.

## Key excerpts

- “Users are becoming increasingly likely to delegate full tasks to Claude, ‘collaborating’ with Claude less.”
- “The implications for the workforce are uncertain.”
- “The rate, scale, and form of AI's economic effects will determine the policy responses that are necessary across the world.”
- “The proposals below don’t necessarily represent Anthropic's own policy positions.”
- Anthropic says taxes in this broader category deserve serious study, even though they could directly affect its own profitability.

## Anthropic’s framing

Anthropic says:
- policy must be robust to uncertainty about timing, scale, and form of disruption
- preparation should begin before the economic shape of AI is fully known
- the policy conversation should draw on economists, policy experts, and multiple ideological perspectives

## Scenario framework

Anthropic organizes ideas into three buckets:
1. Policies for nearly all scenarios
2. Policies for moderate acceleration
3. Policies for faster-moving scenarios

## Example policy ideas surfaced
- worker and student upskilling
- permitting reform for energy and compute infrastructure
- stronger fiscal support for displaced workers
- possible taxes on automation to offset labor externalities
- sovereign wealth funds or broader public stakes in AI revenue for faster-disruption scenarios

## Capture note
This raw file preserves the extracted markdown/summary used for wiki synthesis. It is immutable once captured.

title: # A policy agenda for responsible AI progress: Opportunity, Responsibility, Security
summary: Source URL: https://blog.google/innovation-and-ai/products/a-policy-agenda-for-responsible-ai-progress-opportunity-responsibility-security/
text:
# A policy agenda for responsible AI progress: Opportunity, Responsibility, Security

Source URL: https://blog.google/innovation-and-ai/products/a-policy-agenda-for-responsible-ai-progress-opportunity-responsibility-security/
Publisher: Google
Author: Kent Walker
Date: 2023-05-19
Captured: 2026-04-19
Type: Web article

## Extracted notes

## Core thesis

Google argues that AI development should not be halted. Instead, governance should be built around three pillars: opportunity, responsibility, and security. The piece emphasizes coordinated action across government, companies, universities, and international partners.

## Key excerpts

- “Today Google is publishing a white paper with suggestions for a policy agenda for responsible AI progress.”
- “Calls for a halt to technological advances are unlikely to be successful or effective, and risk missing out on AI’s substantial benefits and falling behind those who embrace its potential.”
- “AI is too important not to regulate, and too important not to regulate well.”

## Google’s policy framing

Google emphasizes:
- private-sector practices plus shared industry standards
- government policy and international coordination
- multi-stakeholder participation across government, academia, civil society, and companies
- public trust as a prerequisite for wide AI adoption

## Three pillars
1. Opportunity
2. Responsibility
3. Security

## Example recommendations
- invest in innovation and competitiveness
- support foundational AI research via national labs and research institutions
- use privacy laws and trusted cross-border data flows to enable responsible innovation
- support continuing education and workforce upskilling
- reduce harms like misinformation, discrimination, and misuse through governance and standards
- address security and geopolitical risks from powerful AI systems

## Capture note
This raw file preserves the extracted markdown/summary used for wiki synthesis. It is immutable once captured.

title: # Codex for (almost) everything — Summary

text:
# Codex for (almost) everything — Summary

Source: https://openai.com/index/codex-for-almost-everything/
Date: 2026-04-16
Captured: 2026-04-19
Type: web article

## Core announcement

OpenAI announced a major Codex update expanding it across the full software development lifecycle and beyond pure coding.

## What’s new
- Computer use: Codex can see, click, and type with its own cursor on the user's computer.
- In-app browser: page-level feedback inside rendered web pages.
- Image generation in workflow with gpt-image-1.5.
- 90+ plugins/integrations including skills and MCP servers.
- Better lifecycle support: PR review comments, multiple terminals, remote devboxes via SSH, rich previews for docs/slides/spreadsheets/PDFs.
- Automations with persistent thread context for recurring and long-running work.
- Preference memory and learning from prior actions.

## Product framing
OpenAI describes Codex as a tool for millions of developers and as a partner across design, building, testing, review, and repeated operational work.

## Key excerpts
> “We’re releasing a major update to Codex, making it a more powerful partner for the more than 3 million developers who use it every week to accelerate work across the full software development lifecycle.”

> “Codex can now operate your computer alongside you, work with more of the tools and apps you use everyday, generate images, remember your preferences, learn from previous actions, and take on ongoing and repeatable work.”

> “Together, these improvements make it faster to move across all the stages of the software development lifecycle between writing code, checking outputs, reviewing changes, and collaborating with the agent in one workspace.”

title: # Introducing Codex — Summary
summary: Source: https://openai.com/index/introducing-codex/
text:
# Introducing Codex — Summary

Source: https://openai.com/index/introducing-codex/
Date: 2025-05-16
Captured: 2026-04-19
Type: web article

## TL;DR

OpenAI introduced Codex, a cloud-based software engineering agent in ChatGPT that can work on many coding tasks in parallel.

## Key points
- Powered by codex-1, described as a software-engineering-optimized version of OpenAI o3.
- Can write features, answer questions about a codebase, fix bugs, propose pull requests, and run tests, linters, and type checkers.
- Each task runs in an isolated cloud sandbox preloaded with a repository.
- OpenAI positioned the workflow as asynchronous and multi-agent.
- Human review remains essential before integrating code.

## Key excerpts
> “A cloud-based software engineering agent that can work on many tasks in parallel, powered by codex-1.”

> “Codex can perform tasks for you such as writing features, answering questions about your codebase, fixing bugs, and proposing pull requests for review; each task runs in its own cloud sandbox environment, preloaded with your repository.”

> “We believe the asynchronous, multi-agent workflow introduced by Codex in ChatGPT will become the de facto way engineers produce high-quality code.”

title: # Introducing GPT-5.3-Codex — Summary
summary: Source: https://openai.com/index/introducing-gpt-5-3-codex/
text:
# Introducing GPT-5.3-Codex — Summary

Source: https://openai.com/index/introducing-gpt-5-3-codex/
Date: 2026-02-05
Captured: 2026-04-19
Type: web article

## Core announcement

OpenAI introduced GPT-5.3-Codex, described as “the most capable agentic coding model to date” and as a step from coding/review toward a broader computer-use professional agent.

## Key points
- Combines coding performance with broader reasoning and professional knowledge.
- OpenAI says it is 25% faster and better on long-running tasks involving research, tool use, and complex execution.
- Supports interactive steering while working.
- OpenAI frames it as progress toward a single, general-purpose agent for technical work.
- OpenAI also says this is the first model it classifies as High capability for cybersecurity-related tasks under its Preparedness Framework.

## Benchmarks highlighted by OpenAI
- SWE-Bench Pro: 56.8%
- Terminal-Bench 2.0: 77.3%
- OSWorld-Verified: 64.7%
- GDPval wins or ties: 70.9%

## Key excerpts
> “We’re introducing a new model that unlocks even more of what Codex can do: GPT‑5.3‑Codex, the most capable agentic coding model to date.”

> “With GPT‑5.3‑Codex, Codex goes from an agent that can write and review code to an agent that can do nearly anything developers and professionals can do on a computer.”

> “Together, these results ... mark a step change toward a single, general-purpose agent that can reason, build, and execute across the full spectrum of real-world technical work.”

title: # OpenClaw homepage — Summary
summary: Source: https://openclaw.ai/
text:
# OpenClaw homepage — Summary

Source: https://openclaw.ai/
Captured: 2026-04-19
Type: web article

## Core message
OpenClaw is an open-source personal AI assistant that runs on the user’s own machine and is accessed through chat apps.

## Key points
- Emphasizes user-controlled infrastructure, persistent memory, browser and system control, skills/plugins, and cross-platform support.
- Positioned as a hackable, on-prem, personal agent rather than an enterprise-hosted coding workspace.
- Supports Anthropic, OpenAI, and local models.

title: # America’s AI Action Plan
summary: Source URL: https://www.whitehouse.gov/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/Americas-AI-Action-Plan.pdf
text:
# America’s AI Action Plan

Source URL: https://www.whitehouse.gov/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/Americas-AI-Action-Plan.pdf
Publisher: The White House
Date: 2025-07
Captured: 2026-04-19
Type: PDF

## Extracted notes

## Core thesis

The plan frames AI as a strategic race for economic, military, and geopolitical dominance. Its stated objective is for the United States to achieve and maintain global AI leadership by accelerating innovation, building infrastructure, and leading internationally in diplomacy and security.

## Key excerpts

- “The United States is in a race to achieve global dominance in artificial intelligence (AI).”
- “We need to build and maintain vast AI infrastructure and the energy to power it.”
- AI should complement Americans’ work rather than replace it.
- Federal procurement should favor systems described as objective and free from top-down ideological bias.

## Main pillars
1. Accelerate AI Innovation
2. Build American AI Infrastructure
3. Lead in International AI Diplomacy and Security

## Dominant themes
- deregulation and removal of barriers viewed as slowing AI progress
- worker-first rhetoric paired with productivity and retraining claims
- ideological neutrality and free-speech framing in federal procurement
- national security, export controls, and geopolitical competition
- data centers, chips, and energy expansion as core state priorities
- exporting the American AI stack to allies while restricting adversaries

## Capture note
This raw file preserves the extracted markdown/summary used for wiki synthesis. It is immutable once captured.

title: # Industrial Policy for the Intelligence Age: Ideas to Keep People First
summary: Source URL: https://cdn.openai.com/pdf/561e7512-253e-424b-9734-ef4098440601/Industrial%20Policy%20for%20the%20Intelligence%20Age.pdf
text:
# Industrial Policy for the Intelligence Age: Ideas to Keep People First

Source URL: https://cdn.openai.com/pdf/561e7512-253e-424b-9734-ef4098440601/Industrial%20Policy%20for%20the%20Intelligence%20Age.pdf
Publisher: OpenAI
Date: 2026-04
Captured: 2026-04-19
Type: PDF

## Extracted notes

OpenAI, April 2026

## Core thesis

OpenAI argues that the transition from today’s frontier AI to superintelligence is already underway and will require a new industrial policy designed to:

1. Share prosperity broadly
2. Mitigate risks
3. Democratize access and agency

The paper frames this as a democratic governance challenge, not just a technical one: policy must keep pace so AI expands access, agency, and opportunity rather than concentrating wealth and power.

## Key excerpts

- “Now, we’re beginning a transition toward superintelligence: AI systems capable of outperforming the smartest humans even when they are assisted by AI.”
- “At OpenAI, we believe we should navigate it through a democratic process that gives people real power to shape the AI future they want...”
- “As capability scales, safety must scale with it.”
- “The transition to superintelligence is not a distant possibility—it's already underway, and the choices we make in the near term will shape how its benefits and risks are distributed for decades to come.”
- “AI data centers should pay their own way on energy so that households aren't subsidizing them; and they should generate local jobs and tax revenue.”
- “This public-private collaboration should stave off regulatory capture and centralized control, instead preserving the freedom to innovate while ensuring that the onset of superintelligence isn't dominated by the most powerful forces in society.”

## Document goals

1. Share prosperity broadly
2. Mitigate risks
3. Democratize access and agency

## OpenAI’s concrete next steps

OpenAI says it is:
- welcoming and organizing feedback through newindustrialpolicy@openai.com
- establishing a pilot program of fellowships and focused research grants of up to $100,000 and up to $1 million in API credits for work that builds on these and related policy ideas
- convening discussions at its new OpenAI Workshop opening in May in Washington, DC

## Executive summary notes

The paper argues that AI is moving from handling tasks that take minutes, to hours, and potentially months, which will transform work, institutions, and production. While OpenAI expects AI’s benefits to outweigh harms, it highlights serious risks:

- job and industry disruption
- misuse by bad actors
- loss of alignment or control
- anti-democratic government or institutional use
- concentration of wealth and power

The proposed response is a new industrial policy agenda combining:

- government tools: research funding, workforce development, regulation, procurement, investment
- non-government experimentation: pilots, measurement, iteration
- public-private collaboration without regulatory capture

The paper is exploratory, focused initially on the United States, but intended to support a global conversation.

## Why a new industrial policy is needed

OpenAI compares the AI transition to prior technological shifts such as the Industrial Age, when democratic societies responded with labor protections, safety standards, social safety nets, and expanded access to education. The claim is that superintelligence will require an even more ambitious update to the social contract.

The paper says industrial policy is justified when:
- technologies create opportunities and risks faster than institutions can adapt
- breakthroughs need help becoming scaled industries
- benefits would otherwise accrue narrowly

## Section notes: building an open economy

Advanced AI could:
- lower the cost of essential goods
- accelerate scientific progress
- increase productivity
- free time for meaningful and relational life
- help solve problems in medicine, food, agriculture, and energy

But it could also:
- eliminate or transform jobs quickly
- reshape industries unevenly
- widen inequality
- concentrate gains in a few firms
- leave workers more productive without sharing the upside

One highlighted policy idea is worker perspectives: giving workers a formal voice in AI deployment decisions to improve job quality and safety while avoiding harmful uses that intensify workloads, reduce autonomy, or undermine fair scheduling and pay.

## Capture note

This raw file preserves the extracted markdown/summary used for wiki synthesis. It is immutable once captured.
